content:
*I ease my knee against my boy’s to still the bounce and slide a finger under the thin ribbon in the psalter as the reader stands. Dust floats in the slant of light from the south windows; his hair glows where it catches it.*

"When you hear it," I whisper, "listen with your eyes too."

The voice at the front begins: "The heavens declare the glory of God…" I tilt my head toward the high panes. We don’t make a show of it. Just a look up, like you do when a bird cuts across the sky. When the line comes about the sun "like a bridegroom… runs his course with joy," I trace a small arc across his palm with my thumb — east to west — and close his fingers around it. He grins, because it tickles, and then sits still.

I don’t start a quiet lecture. I let the Psalm work. The law of the Lord is perfect, reviving the soul — I let that line land in me too. When we stand for the hymn, I put my hand on his shoulder for a beat. "See it?" I murmur as we sit again. "The sky says the same thing the page says." He nods, serious as only ten can be.

After the benediction, I don’t pick a fight in the aisle. "Walk with me," I tell him, and we slip out the side door. In the yard I shade my eyes and point. "There’s where it came up," I say, marking the low east with my hand, "and there’s where it’ll finish." He squints along my line. A swallow stitches the air overhead. "God set it to run that way," I say, "and then told us so. Poetry doesn’t make it less true; it makes it sing while it tells the truth." He tries the sentence back at me — a little crooked — and I don’t correct him; I smile and let the trying stand.

I keep the unity we were asked for by not turning the narthex into a court. But I don’t trade my son’s clarity for anybody’s calm. Midweek, I ask the elder for coffee. "When you say ‘poetic,’" I ask him plain, "do you mean ‘not real,’ or ‘real in a key that sings’? Because my boy sits under both the sky and the Scripture every Lord’s Day, and I won’t teach him to hear them against each other." I keep my tone soft and my spine straight. If he calls Genesis 1 poetry, I can nod at the artistry; if he means ‘therefore it didn’t happen as said,’ I tell him I can’t teach my child to treat God’s words as plastic just to make grown-ups comfortable.

Next Sunday, when Psalm 19 comes round again in our mouths or our memory, I’ll do the same simple thing: look up, trace the arc, let the words and the world agree where a boy can see it.
